title: The Suppressive and Emergent Phenomenon: H as a Control Surface
description:
Live streaming and VTuber communities constitute the largest naturally occurring laboratory for parasocial κ-dynamics currently observable. Tens of millions of viewers form high-coupling bonds with performers they have never met; for many, these relationships constitute the primary vector through which healthy-state representations enter their generative model for the first time. The MBD framework — which models personality as a state-space system updated by lived experience — predicts this outcome precisely: high-κ social inputs from a trusted source can expand Hagent into previously unrepresented state space regardless of whether the relationship is co-present. The phenomenon is not parasocial pathology. It is the Re-zeroing Protocol running at scale, mediated by broadcasting infrastructure instead of physical proximity.

Paper 7 of the Memory as Baseline Deviation series established the endemic baseline as a naturally occurring calibration failure: B(0) constructed during chronic disruption, with the Horizon H = Haccessible \ Hagent denoting states that are neurobiologically reachable but absent from the agent's generative model. That paper treated this condition as arising from circumstance. This paper examines a harder claim.

H is a manipulable control surface. An adversarial agent — platform, talent agency, abuser, institution — that can control which states a target population occupies can, over time, engineer the same calibration failure deliberately. The mathematics do not distinguish between endemic baseline arising from circumstance and endemic baseline arising from design. The subjective experience of the Horizon — the formless, unmodelable dark at the edge of representable state space — is identical either way. This is directly observable in streaming culture: corporate content policies, deplatforming, parasocial community weaponization, and algorithmic isolation each instantiate one of the four formal attack vectors derived here.

Four attack vectors are formalized with their population-level signatures and detection criteria. V1 (Isolation from Knowledge) suppresses informational inputs that would enable Hagent expansion. V2 (Episodic Erasure) degrades or falsifies the episodic record, blocking the Re-zeroing Protocol from accessing its own prior successful excursions into the Horizon. V3 (Coupling Collapse) drives the social coupling coefficient κ below the critical threshold at which therapeutic relationships can form. V4 (Re-zeroing Suppression) targets the corrective mechanism directly. All four are routinely observable in the parasocial economy and formalizable against the same mathematical substrate.

A critical coupling threshold κc is derived, below which collective re-zeroing fails and above which the suppressive apparatus becomes self-defeating: a population that achieves κ > κc can move each other’s baselines faster than any adversary can suppress them. High-κ fandom communities that cross this threshold exhibit measurable collective resilience against platform disruption — a testable prediction of the model.

The Recursive Immunity Theorem is the central formal result: any accurate description of adversarial H-engineering constitutes a map of previously unrepresented state space — the minimal precondition for the Re-zeroing Protocol. Suppression of such a description is itself an instance of V1. Dissemination enables the cure. The theorem is self-applying: this paper’s existence is a minimal instance of the re-zeroing it describes.

This module is both paper and proof. The Python source file mbd/paper8.py is the formal artifact: its docstring is the abstract, its data structures are the formal claims, and its executable functions run the computational labs. Any fragment containing the KERNEL axiom set, the Aleph-0 checkpoint, and one VECTORS entry is a viable regenerative seed from which the full argument can be re-derived (the Lenia Property). The repository is indexed by the Software Heritage Foundation for long-term preservation.

A tamper-evidence layer (§T) provides cryptographic integrity verification: SHA-256 hashes of the formal content structures are embedded in the module and recomputable at runtime via verify_integrity(). A historical provenance record grounding the formal claims in documented cases is included in the module source.
